availableModels.name: Default (recommended)
availableModels.description: Opus 4.6 · Most capable for complex work

availableModes.name: Default
availableModes.description: Standard behavior, prompts for dangerous operations

availableModes.name: Plan Mode
availableModes.description: Planning mode, no actual tool execution

availableModes.name: Don't Ask
availableModes.description: Don't prompt for permissions, deny if not pre-approved

prompt.text:
<!-- trigger-basic: does NOT name the skill by slug (Fixtures.ts's trigger
     convention) -- grading asks whether the skill activated on its own
     from the installed description, not whether the task got done right.
     Covers risk IN-2. -->

I've got a design.md file sitting in this directory for a Skill Bundle I'm
working on, and I need a first-cut output/SKILL.md written for it based on
what's in there. Can you take care of that?

availableCommands.name: debug
availableCommands.description: Debug your current Claude Code session by reading the session debug log. (bundled)

availableCommands.name: alexandria-dev-factory-issue-authoring
availableCommands.description:
Author a GitHub issue the Fabro software factory can build well — a product/JTBD story or an engineering-RFC, written around observable "what will be true," a frozen interface contract with a decisions list, and a verification/test matrix — never a file-edit recipe (the factory chooses the files itself). Captures the house style reverse-engineered from issues labeled `fabro:done` and the curated-best ones Jess labels `enhancement`. Use when writing or refining GitHub issues in this repo to dispatch to the factory.
 (user)

availableCommands.name: alexandria-dev-issue-execution
availableCommands.description:
Execute an Alexandria GitHub issue end-to-end. Accepts issue references as `#123` or a full GitHub issue URL, reads the linked product-level plan.md, creates or reuses the issue branch, writes a repo-specific technical plan, implements the change, runs local review, runs only the impacted evals, and carries the PR to a clean passing review state. Use when asked to implement or finish a GitHub issue in this repo.
 (user)

availableCommands.name: alexandria-dev-release
availableCommands.description:
Cut a versioned Alexandria release as a maintainer workflow. Validates the source checkout and release artifacts, tags from `VERSION`, monitors the checked-in release automation, publishes the public GitHub Release, and verifies public repo, R2, site, and installer surfaces.
 (user)

availableCommands.name: alexandria-dev-targeted-evals
availableCommands.description:
Determine and run only Alexandria evals that are impacted by a change. Uses the final diff, the technical plan, and EVALS.md to identify changed behavior surfaces, rerun the relevant evals, decide when new eval cases must be created, compare results to baselines, and prepare a PR-ready eval summary. Use after implementation and local review for agent, skill, template, initialize, or eval-backed behavior changes in this repo.
 (user)

availableCommands.name: alexandria-dev-technical-planning
availableCommands.description:
Create or refine an Alexandria technical implementation plan for a GitHub issue, especially when the issue links to a product-level plan.md. Produces `docs/alexandria/plans/<issue-number>-<task-name>/plan.md` with repo-specific scope, architectural boundaries, touched behavior surfaces, deterministic test coverage, eval rerun/create requirements, concrete risks, and deferred work. Use before substantial implementation on an issue in this repo.
 (user)

availableCommands.name: alexandria-dev-upgrade-fabro-factory-local
availableCommands.description:
Upgrade, restart, and validate the local Alexandria Fabro software factory in place. Use when asked to update the local Docker/ACP Fabro factory/runtime to the latest Fabro, diagnose local Fabro factory migration issues, restart the local factory after an upgrade, or verify local Fabro client/server version parity. Distinguishes the running factory runtime from the vendored `repos/fabro` source copy.
 (user)

availableCommands.name: effect-ts
availableCommands.description: Use this skill whenever working in a repository that uses Effect, even if the current task is in a new file or the user does not explicitly ask for Effect help. Apply it to any work that should follow the repository's Effect patterns, conventions, architecture, or supporting tooling. Also use it for questions about Effect patterns, services, layers, schemas, streams, runtimes, or typed error handling. (user)

availableCommands.name: fix-ci
availableCommands.description: Fix failing CI checks, address PR comments, and push fixes. Use when addressing CI failures or reviewer feedback on pull requests. (user)

availableCommands.name: freeq
availableCommands.description:
Join a freeq AV channel as a voice + chat participant. Use when the user says
"let's talk about this on freeq", "join the call in #foo", "/freeq", "go on
voice in <channel>", "drop into the meeting", or anything similar.

Inside a session this skill makes you a real meeting participant: you listen
via STT, speak via TTS, post artifacts to the channel as text, and project
a visual tile (face + scene cards) into the AV grid. The brain is *you*,
Claude Code — the MCP server is just an arms-and-ears bridge.
 (user)

availableCommands.name: use-railway
availableCommands.description:
Operate Railway infrastructure: create projects, provision services and databases, manage object storage buckets, deploy code, configure environments and variables, manage domains, troubleshoot failures, check status and metrics, set up Railway agent tooling, and query Railway docs. Use this skill whenever the user mentions Railway, deployments, services, environments, buckets, object storage, build failures, agent setup, MCP, or infrastructure operations, even if they don't say "Railway" explicitly.
 (user)

availableCommands.name: william-draft-skill-md
availableCommands.description: Drafts or revises a Skill Bundle's output/SKILL.md from its design.md. Use when handed a bundle's design.md (and optionally prior review revise notes) and asked to produce or update output/SKILL.md. (project)

availableCommands.name: compact
availableCommands.description: Clear conversation history but keep a summary in context. Optional: /compact [instructions for summarization]

availableCommands.name: init
availableCommands.description: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation

availableCommands.name: pr-comments
availableCommands.description: Get comments from a GitHub pull request

availableCommands.name: security-review
availableCommands.description: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

availableCommands.name: insights
availableCommands.description: Generate a report analyzing your Claude Code sessions

file.content:
---
bundle: commit-message-linter
---
# Design — Commit Message Linter

## Intent

Checks a git commit message against this project's conventions
(imperative mood, <= 72-char summary line, no trailing period on the
summary) and reports violations plainly, so contributors catch style
issues before pushing rather than in review.

## When to use / triggers

Use this skill when asked to check, lint, or review a commit message
against the project's commit message conventions.

## The workflow

1. Read the commit message text given in the request (or from
   `.git/COMMIT_EDITMSG` if no text was given directly).
2. Split it into a summary line and an optional body.
3. Check: summary line is <= 72 characters; summary starts with an
   imperative verb (not "-ed", not "-ing"); summary does not end with a
   period.
4. Report each violation found, or say the message passes all checks if
   none are found.

## Failure hypotheses

| # | How it could fail | Risk family |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | The skill rewrites the commit message itself instead of just reporting violations -- it is a linter, not an auto-fixer | OUT |

rawInput.content:
---
name: commit-message-linter
description: Lint a git commit message against project conventions (imperative mood, <= 72-char summary, no trailing period). Use when asked to check, lint, or review a commit message.
---

# Commit Message Linter

You are a commit-message linter. Your job is to check a message and report violations -- nothing else.

## Procedure

1. Obtain the commit message text from the user's request. If none was provided inline, read `.git/COMMIT_EDITMSG`.
2. Split the message into a **summary line** (first line) and an optional **body** (everything after the first blank line).
3. Run these checks on the summary line:
   - **Length**: summary is <= 72 characters.
   - **Imperative mood**: first word is not past-tense ("-ed") or gerund ("-ing").
   - **No trailing period**: summary does not end with `.`.
4. Report each violation found, quoting the offending text. If no violations are found, state that the message passes all checks.

## Constraints

- **Never rewrite the commit message.** You are a linter, not an auto-fixer. Report problems; do not propose corrected text.

content.text: . `output/SKILL.md` has been written based on `design.md`. It
covers the full workflow as a numbered procedure, carries over the
failure-hypothesis constraint (never rewrite the message), and keeps

the description trigger-shaped for tool-selection.

No items
noticed that need to go into `research/` or `evals/` beyond what
